in_scope_eips: EIP-7823: Set upper bounds for MODEXP
EIP-7918: Blob base fee bounded by execution cost
EIP-7934: RLP Execution Block Size Limit
EIP-7935: Set default gas limit to XX0M
EIP-7951: Precompile for secp256r1 Curve Support
scope_notes: Only Fusaka-specific code is in scope of this competition.
Vulnerabilities requiring the user to have publicly exposed an API (JSON-RPC, Beacon API) are explicitly out-of-scope.
High-effort single-peer DoS attacks (sustained, CPU or bandwidth intensive, requires more than 1 packet or on-chain transaction) are out-of-scope.
All submissions (except informational) must include an executable coded proof of concept at the time of submission.
Severity is measured by the finding's impact on the entire Ethereum network.

actors.name: Transaction Sender
actors.description: External user or contract that submits transactions to the Ethereum network, including standard transactions and EIP-7702 set-code transactions with Authorization payloads. The transaction sender controls the nonce, balance transfers, code deployment, and authorization signatures.

actors.name: Deposit Contract
actors.description: The system-level deposit contract (at DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS) that emits deposit events consumed by the execution layer during block processing. This is a pre-deployed contract whose address is a protocol constant.

actors.name: Block Producer / Validator
actors.description: The entity that produces blocks containing transactions and their receipts. The block producer determines transaction ordering and inclusion, and the receipts trie is constructed from execution results.

actors.name: Execution Layer Node
actors.description: The local execution layer client that processes state transitions, encodes accounts (RLP encoding with keccak256), validates deposit requests from logs, and computes the requests hash (SHA-256). This is the core processing entity for the analyzed specifications.

actors.name: Consensus Layer Node
actors.description: The consensus layer client that receives the requests hash from the execution layer via the Engine API and uses it for consensus validation. The CL relies on the EL to correctly compute the requests hash.

actors.name: P2P Network Peer
actors.description: Any node on the Ethereum P2P network that relays blocks, transactions, and other protocol messages. P2P peers are unauthenticated and may send arbitrary or malicious data.

actors.name: EIP-7702 Authorizer
actors.description: An account holder who signs an Authorization tuple (chain_id, address, nonce) with their ECDSA private key to delegate code execution rights via EIP-7702 set-code transactions. The authorizer's signature (y_parity, r, s) grants another address the ability to set code on their behalf.

trust_boundaries.description: Transaction submission to the execution layer for processing. Includes standard transactions and EIP-7702 set-code transactions containing Authorization payloads with ECDSA signatures. The transaction sender controls the payload content, signatures, nonces, and balances.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Transaction payload, EIP-7702 Authorization tuples (chain_id, address, nonce, y_parity, r, s), gas parameters, value transfers

trust_boundaries.description: Deposit event log data emitted by the deposit contract, parsed by the EL during block processing via parse_deposit_requests and extract_deposit_data. The EL filters logs by DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS and DEPOSIT_EVENT_SIGNATURE_HASH, then extracts structured deposit data (pubkey, withdrawal_credentials, amount, signature, index).
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Deposit event log data (576 bytes ABI-encoded): pubkey (48 bytes), withdrawal_credentials (32 bytes), amount (8 bytes), signature (96 bytes), index (8 bytes)

trust_boundaries.description: Block data provided by the block producer, including the receipts trie from which deposit request logs are extracted. The receipts are retrieved via trie_get using receipt_keys, maintaining ordering. A malicious block producer could craft receipts to influence deposit request parsing.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Block headers, receipts trie, receipt keys (ordered), transaction results

trust_boundaries.description: The execution layer computes the requests hash (via compute_requests_hash using SHA-256) and passes it to the consensus layer via the Engine API. The CL uses this hash for block validation and consensus. If the EL computes an incorrect hash, the CL may accept or reject blocks incorrectly.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Requests hash (SHA-256 digest), deposit requests data, block validation results

trust_boundaries.description: Blocks and transactions received from P2P network peers. These include encoded accounts (RLP-encoded with keccak256 code hashes), deposit contract interactions, and EIP-7702 authorizations. P2P peers are unauthenticated and can send arbitrary malformed data.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Blocks containing RLP-encoded accounts, deposit event logs, Authorization tuples, transaction payloads

trust_boundaries.description: EIP-7702 Authorization signatures submitted as part of set-code transactions. The authorizer signs a tuple of (chain_id, address, nonce) which grants code-setting authority. The EL must verify the ECDSA signature (y_parity, r, s) and validate chain_id matching and nonce correctness.
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Authorization signatures (y_parity, r, s), chain_id, delegated address, authorizer nonce

trust_boundaries.description: Internal state transitions within the EL including account encoding (encode_account), type definitions, and EMPTY_ACCOUNT constant construction. These are internal operations but process data that may originate from untrusted sources (e.g., account code from transactions).
trust_boundaries.data_flow: Account state (nonce, balance, code, storage_root), RLP-encoded account data, keccak256 code hashes

assumptions.text: The deposit contract at DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS is correctly deployed and emits well-formed deposit events. However, the event data is ABI-encoded and must still be rigorously validated (offsets, sizes) because any transaction can trigger log emissions that appear to come from this address if the contract is called with crafted input.

The RLP encoding order for account serialization is strictly (nonce, balance, storage_root, keccak256(code)). Any deviation in encoding order would cause state root mismatches leading to consensus failures. All EL implementations must follow this exact ordering.

The keccak256 hash function used for code hashing in encode_account is collision-resistant. A collision would allow two different code bytecodes to produce the same account encoding, potentially leading to state confusion.

The ECDSA signature (y_parity, r, s) in EIP-7702 Authorization is verified against the authorizing account before any code delegation occurs. If signature verification is bypassed or incorrectly implemented, an attacker could set arbitrary code on any account.

The chain_id in EIP-7702 Authorization must match the target chain to prevent cross-chain replay attacks. If chain_id validation is missing or incorrect, a valid authorization on one chain could be replayed on another.

The nonce in EIP-7702 Authorization must match the current nonce of the authorizing account. This prevents replay of previously-used authorizations. If nonce validation is skipped, old authorizations could be reused.

Deposit requests are processed in receipt_keys order, preserving the ordering of deposits within a block. Any reordering could cause disagreement between EL and CL about the deposit sequence, leading to consensus failures or missed deposits.

The compute_requests_hash function uses SHA-256, and the hash is order-dependent. All EL implementations must process requests in the same order to produce identical hashes. The hash of an empty request list produces sha256() of empty input.

The extract_deposit_data function strictly validates all five offsets and five sizes against expected constants before extracting data. Any validation bypass could allow malformed deposit data to be included in the requests hash, causing consensus divergence between clients.

The Engine API communication between EL and CL is authenticated via JWT. A compromised or buggy EL could send incorrect requests hashes to the CL, potentially causing the CL to finalize incorrect state.

P2P peers are completely untrusted. All block and transaction data received via P2P must be fully validated before being processed. This includes verifying account encoding, deposit data parsing, authorization signatures, and requests hash computation.

Type aliases (Address = Bytes20, Root = Hash32, VersionedHash = Hash32, Bloom = Bytes256) enforce fixed-size constraints. If any component accepts values that violate these size constraints, it could cause buffer overflows, truncation, or state corruption.

EMPTY_ACCOUNT is a module-level frozen constant with nonce=0, balance=0, code=b''. If this constant is mutated or if comparison against it fails, empty account detection could break, causing state bloat or incorrect pruning.
trie_get must return a non-None receipt for every key in receipt_keys. If a receipt is missing or corrupted, the deposit parsing could skip deposits or crash, leading to consensus divergence.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could forge an EIP-7702 Authorization by crafting ECDSA signatures (y_parity, r, s) that appear to authorize code delegation from a victim's account. If the signature verification is flawed (e.g., accepting malleable signatures, not checking s-value range, or recovering to an unexpected address), the attacker could impersonate any account and set arbitrary code on it.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The EL must strictly verify ECDSA signatures over the authorization tuple (chain_id, address, nonce). The s-value must be in the lower half of the curve order to prevent malleability. The recovered address must exactly match the authorizing account.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could replay an EIP-7702 Authorization from one chain on another chain if chain_id validation is missing or incorrect. The Authorization tuple includes chain_id specifically for replay protection, but if the EL does not validate chain_id against the current chain, cross-chain replay is possible.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The EL must verify that the chain_id in the Authorization matches the current chain's ID. A chain_id of 0 may be treated specially (wildcard), which must be documented and handled correctly.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could replay a previously-used EIP-7702 Authorization if nonce validation is missing. The Authorization includes the authorizer's nonce to ensure one-time use. If the nonce is not checked against the account's current nonce, old authorizations could be reused after the account has transacted.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The EL must verify that the nonce in the Authorization matches the current nonce of the authorizing account. The nonce must be incremented after successful authorization to prevent replay.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious transaction sender could craft inputs to the deposit contract that produce log events with valid DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS and DEPOSIT_EVENT_SIGNATURE_HASH but contain malformed ABI-encoded data. The extract_deposit_data function validates offsets and sizes, but if any validation check is incomplete (e.g., integer overflow in offset calculations, off-by-one in size checks), malformed deposit data could enter the requests hash.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The extract_deposit_data function must strictly validate: (1) total data length equals DEPOSIT_EVENT_LENGTH (576 bytes), (2) all five offsets match expected constants, (3) all five sizes match expected constants. Every validation failure must raise InvalidBlock. The function correctly implements these checks per the subgraph.

stride_analysis.description: If the offset or size validation in extract_deposit_data uses Uint.from_be_bytes for parsing offsets, integer overflow or sign confusion could allow an attacker to bypass validation. For example, if the offset value wraps around due to large values, the extraction could read data from unexpected positions in the log data.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Offset and size values must be parsed as unsigned integers and compared against exact expected constants. The Python specification uses Uint which is arbitrary-precision and avoids overflow, but implementation languages (Go, Rust, Java) may need explicit bounds checking.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious block producer could craft a receipts trie where receipt_keys are ordered in a way that changes the deposit ordering. Since deposit_requests are accumulated in receipt_keys order (INV-008), any inconsistency in key ordering between EL implementations could cause different deposit sequences, leading to different requests hashes and consensus divergence.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Receipt keys must be iterated in a canonical order (RLP-encoded transaction index). All EL implementations must use the same key ordering to ensure identical deposit request sequences.

stride_analysis.description: If the compute_requests_hash function processes requests in a different order across EL implementations, the resulting SHA-256 hash will differ. Since the hash is order-dependent (INV-012), any ordering discrepancy between implementations would cause consensus splits between clients running different EL software.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The requests list must be processed in a deterministic, canonical order defined by the specification. The double-SHA-256 construction (individual SHA-256 per request, then outer SHA-256 over digests) must be implemented identically across all clients.

stride_analysis.description: If the RLP encoding order in encode_account deviates from the specification (nonce, balance, storage_root, keccak256(code)), the state root will differ between implementations. A wrong field order would cause silent state divergence that only manifests when nodes compare state roots, potentially causing a network split.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The RLP encoding order must be strictly (nonce, balance, storage_root, keccak256(code)) as defined in INV-003. This is a fundamental invariant that all EL implementations must respect.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious P2P peer could send blocks containing a very large number of deposit events, forcing the EL to perform extensive validation in extract_deposit_data for each event. Each deposit event requires 10+ conditional checks (5 offset validations, 5 size validations). A block stuffed with deposit contract calls could cause excessive CPU usage during block processing.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Gas limits naturally bound the number of deposit events per block. However, the validation loop is linear in the number of events. If the per-event validation cost is high relative to gas cost, this could be exploitable. The existing gas mechanism provides some protection.

stride_analysis.description: A crafted deposit event with invalid data length, offsets, or sizes will cause extract_deposit_data to raise InvalidBlock, rejecting the entire block. An attacker who can cause the deposit contract to emit malformed events could force block rejection, disrupting block production.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The deposit contract is a system contract with fixed behavior. Normal calls should always produce well-formed events. However, if the contract has a bug or can be called in unexpected ways that produce malformed logs, this could cause block rejection. The validation is correct by design - rejecting malformed data is the intended behavior.

stride_analysis.description: A malicious P2P peer could send blocks with malformed RLP-encoded accounts that cause parsing errors or excessive memory allocation during decode. If the RLP decoder does not limit input size or handle malformed data gracefully, it could crash the node or cause excessive resource consumption.
stride_analysis.mitigation: RLP decoders must enforce strict size limits and validate all input. The type aliases (Address=Bytes20, Root=Hash32, etc.) provide size constraints that should be enforced during deserialization.

stride_analysis.description: A transaction sender could craft an EIP-7702 set-code transaction that abuses the Authorization mechanism to set arbitrary code on a victim's account. If the Authorization signature verification has edge cases (e.g., ecrecover returning address(0), accepting invalid curve points, or s-value malleability), the attacker could gain code execution authority over accounts they do not control.
stride_analysis.mitigation: ECDSA signature verification must reject signatures that recover to address(0), must enforce s-value in lower half of curve order, and must verify the recovered address matches the expected authorizer. The nonce and chain_id checks provide additional protection layers.

stride_analysis.description: The deposit event parsing exposes the structure of deposit data including pubkey (48 bytes), withdrawal_credentials (32 bytes), amount (8 bytes), signature (96 bytes), and index (8 bytes). While this data is inherently public on-chain, incorrect parsing (e.g., reading beyond buffer boundaries) could leak adjacent memory contents in implementation languages without memory safety.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Buffer bounds must be strictly enforced when extracting data from log events. The Python specification naturally handles this, but C/C++ or Go implementations must ensure no out-of-bounds reads occur during data extraction.

stride_analysis.description: If the requests hash computation is non-deterministic or implementation-dependent, a node could claim it computed a different hash than what was actually produced. Since the hash serves as a commitment between EL and CL, any ambiguity in the computation could lead to disputes about block validity that cannot be resolved.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The compute_requests_hash function uses a deterministic algorithm (SHA-256 of SHA-256 digests in list order). The specification is unambiguous: hash each request individually, then update an outer hash with each individual digest in order. This determinism prevents repudiation.

stride_analysis.description: If the keccak256 hash of account code is used as the sole identifier for code content (as in encode_account), a hash collision would allow two different code bytecodes to be treated as identical. While keccak256 collisions are computationally infeasible with current technology, this is a theoretical concern for long-term security.
stride_analysis.mitigation: keccak256 provides 256-bit collision resistance, making practical collisions infeasible. No additional mitigation is needed at current security levels.

stride_analysis.description: An attacker could deploy a contract at a different address that emits events with the same DEPOSIT_EVENT_SIGNATURE_HASH topic. The parse_deposit_requests function correctly filters by both DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS and topic hash, but if the address check is missing or bypassable, spoofed deposit events from arbitrary contracts could be included in the deposit requests.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The parse_deposit_requests function checks both log.address == DEPOSIT_CONTRACT_ADDRESS and log.topics[0] == DEPOSIT_EVENT_SIGNATURE_HASH. Both checks are necessary and sufficient to prevent deposit event spoofing.

stride_analysis.description: If trie_get returns None for a key in receipt_keys (violating INV-010), the EL could crash or enter an undefined state during deposit parsing. A malicious block producer who can craft a block where the receipts trie is inconsistent with receipt_keys could cause nodes processing the block to crash.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The EL must validate that trie_get returns a non-None value for every key in receipt_keys. If a receipt is missing, the block must be rejected as invalid rather than crashing.

stride_analysis.description: A transaction sender submitting EIP-7702 transactions controls all fields including the Authorization list. If the EL processes multiple authorizations within a single transaction without properly handling conflicts (e.g., two authorizations for the same account with different code addresses), the resulting state could be implementation-dependent.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The specification must define clear precedence rules for multiple authorizations within a single transaction. Implementations must handle duplicate authorizer addresses deterministically.

stride_analysis.description: The compute_requests_hash uses a double-SHA-256 construction where each request is individually hashed before being fed to the outer hash. If an implementation incorrectly concatenates raw requests before hashing (single SHA-256 instead of double), the hash would differ, causing consensus divergence. The distinction between sha256(sha256(r1) || sha256(r2)) and sha256(r1 || r2) is critical.
stride_analysis.mitigation: Implementations must follow the exact double-SHA-256 construction: initialize outer sha256, for each request compute sha256(request).digest(), then update outer hash with each digest. The specification is explicit about this construction.

stride_analysis.description: The EMPTY_ACCOUNT constant is defined with nonce=0, balance=0, code=b''. If this constant is defined as mutable (not frozen/immutable) in an implementation, it could be accidentally modified during runtime, causing incorrect empty account detection and potential state corruption.
stride_analysis.mitigation: The EMPTY_ACCOUNT must be defined as a frozen/immutable constant (INV-001). Implementations should use language-level immutability (e.g., frozen dataclass in Python, const in Go, etc.).

stride_analysis.description: Type aliases define strict size constraints (Address=Bytes20, Root=Hash32, VersionedHash=Hash32, Bloom=Bytes256). If an EL implementation does not enforce these size constraints during deserialization of P2P data, oversized or undersized values could cause buffer issues, state corruption, or consensus divergence between implementations that enforce sizes differently.
stride_analysis.mitigation: All implementations must enforce the exact byte sizes defined by type aliases during deserialization. Address must be exactly 20 bytes, Root and VersionedHash must be exactly 32 bytes, and Bloom must be exactly 256 bytes.
